_purpose: Cultural marker profile for the OH Southeast Appalachian cluster (Washington, Athens, Meigs, Gallia, Lawrence, Scioto, Jackson, Vinton). Maps the cultural fingerprints that appear in records and family memory — church denomination as origin and class indicator, naming conventions, foodways, dialect, social hierarchies, material culture, and cultural calendar. Southeastern Ohio was two cultures sharing one landscape: New England institutional culture in Marietta and Athens, and Southern Appalachian hill culture everywhere else, with a Welsh enclave in Jackson/Gallia and immigrant coal camp communities layered on top.
display_name: OH Southeast Appalachian — Cultural Marker Profile

denomination_map.origin_signal: Congregational and Presbyterian congregations in Washington and Athens counties trace directly to the Ohio Company New England migration. The First Congregational Church of Marietta (organized 1796) was the first Protestant church in the Northwest Territory. Presbyterian congregations followed, particularly those connected to Ohio University in Athens. Congregational affiliation in southeastern Ohio is a nearly certain signal of New England origin.
denomination_map.class_signal: The highest-status denomination in Washington County. Congregational and Presbyterian membership signaled the Ohio Company landowning elite — the Putnams, Cutlers, and their descendants who governed the county. By the late nineteenth century, these congregations served the professional and merchant class of Marietta and Athens.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Excellent records. Congregational and Presbyterian session records in Washington and Athens counties are among the best-maintained in Ohio — baptisms, marriages, deaths, disciplinary actions, and letters of dismission. Records held at Marietta College, Ohio University, and the Ohio History Connection.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Baptist congregations were the dominant denomination of the Virginia-Kentucky migration stream. A Baptist affiliation in southeastern Ohio (outside of Washington County's New England core) signals Southern Appalachian origin with high reliability. The Baptist churches were the first institutions established in the hill country — often before schools, courthouses, or stores.
denomination_map.class_signal: Baptist was the religion of the hill-country yeoman and the coal camp worker. Unlike the Congregational/Presbyterian professional class, Baptist membership signaled the farming and working class. Some variation: town Baptist churches ('First Baptist' in county seats) served a slightly more prosperous constituency than hollow or mining camp Baptist churches.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Record-keeping varied enormously. Some Baptist congregations maintained detailed minute books; others kept almost nothing. Association records (collections of minutes from multiple congregations) are often better preserved than individual church records. Check the Ohio Baptist Convention archives and the American Baptist Historical Society.
denomination_map.race_note: Separate Black Baptist congregations were established in Portsmouth, Ironton, and Gallipolis after the Civil War. These became institutional anchors for the small Black communities in the region.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Methodist circuit riders reached southeastern Ohio by the 1800s. Methodist affiliation does not strongly signal geographic origin — Methodism drew from both New England and Virginia-Kentucky migration streams. However, Methodist circuit rider records are among the most geographically detailed church records in the region, as circuit riders documented every community they visited.
denomination_map.class_signal: Methodist membership in southeastern Ohio was a modest upward class signal compared to Baptist — particularly in county seats. Town Methodist churches served merchants and professionals. Mining camp and rural Methodist congregations served the working class.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Ohio Conference records are well-preserved — quarterly conference minutes, circuit rider journals, and membership rolls. These are held at Ohio Wesleyan University and the Ohio History Connection. Circuit rider journals are particularly valuable because they document communities that left no other institutional records.
denomination_map.race_note: AME (African Methodist Episcopal) congregations existed in Portsmouth and Ironton.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Welsh Calvinistic Methodist and Welsh Congregational affiliation is a nearly certain signal of Welsh origin — specifically from Cardiganshire, Carmarthenshire, or Glamorganshire. These denominations were transplanted directly from Welsh chapels to Ohio. Services conducted in Welsh until the late nineteenth century. The denomination is the single most reliable ethnic identifier for the Jackson/Gallia Welsh community.
denomination_map.class_signal: Within the Welsh community, chapel membership was universal and did not strongly differentiate class. Between the Welsh community and the surrounding population, Welsh chapel membership signaled a literate, close-knit ethnic enclave with strong educational values — the Welsh placed high value on education, literacy, and musical achievement.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Welsh church records are exceptionally valuable — many kept in Welsh, with detailed membership rolls, baptisms, and marriages. Some records held at the Ohio History Connection and Jackson County Historical Society. The Welsh tradition of literary culture means that published accounts, eisteddfod records, and biographical sketches supplement the church records.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Catholic affiliation in the coal fields signals immigrant origin — Irish, Polish, Hungarian, Italian, or other Southern/Eastern European background. Catholic parishes in mining towns (Chauncey, Glouster, Portsmouth) were established by and for immigrant miners. In a region otherwise dominated by Protestant denominations, Catholic affiliation is a strong signal of immigrant coal-mining background.
denomination_map.class_signal: Working class in the mining era. Catholic immigrants occupied the same tier as native-born miners but faced additional discrimination based on ethnicity and religion. Catholic membership in the coal fields signaled the newest arrivals — families who had been in America for one or two generations.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Parish records (baptisms, marriages, deaths) are generally well-preserved and held by the Diocese of Columbus or the Diocese of Steubenville. These records are particularly valuable for immigrant families whose origins are otherwise difficult to trace. Records may be in Latin, Polish, Hungarian, or Italian.

denomination_map.origin_signal: Primitive Baptist and Old Regular Baptist congregations in southeastern Ohio signal the deepest Appalachian hill-country origin — families from the most isolated communities in Virginia, Kentucky, and West Virginia. These denominations rejected missionary societies, Sunday schools, and instrumental music. Their presence marks communities of extreme isolation and cultural conservatism.
denomination_map.class_signal: The most isolated, poorest communities in the cluster. Primitive Baptist affiliation signals families at the economic and geographic margins — the ridge-top farms and deep hollows that the mainstream Baptist and Methodist circuit riders reached last.
denomination_map.genealogical_significance: Minimal formal record-keeping. Some congregations maintained minute books; most relied on oral tradition. Research depends on association minutes (where they survive) and community memory. The Ohio History Connection holds some Primitive Baptist association minutes.

naming_conventions.pattern: New England naming in the Ohio Company settlement
naming_conventions.description: Ohio Company settlers brought New England naming conventions: Biblical names (Samuel, Abigail, Rufus, Manasseh), classical names (Cyrus, Julius, Minerva), and family names passed through generations with precision. First sons named for fathers or paternal grandfathers. Middle names used routinely. The naming style was more formal and more varied than the Virginia-Kentucky pattern in the hill country. Distinctive New England names — Rufus, Manasseh, Ebenezer, Ephraim — are diagnostic for Ohio Company settler families in Washington County.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The New England naming pattern was systematic enough to be genealogically useful. A 'Rufus Putnam' in Washington County records signals a direct connection to the founding Putnam family. Classical and Biblical names that would be unusual in the hill country (Manasseh, Ephraim, Cyrus) are reliable New England origin signals.

naming_conventions.pattern: Virginia-Kentucky backcountry naming in the hill country
naming_conventions.description: Virginia and Kentucky migrants brought Scots-Irish and backcountry Southern naming conventions: shorter, simpler given names — John, James, William, Thomas (male); Mary, Elizabeth, Sarah, Nancy (female). First sons often named for paternal grandfather, but the pattern was less rigid than the New England system. Surnames as middle names were less common than in the Carolinas. The use of nicknames was more casual — 'Jim' for James, 'Bill' for William, 'Polly' for Mary — and these nicknames often appear in official records, making identification difficult.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The informality of hill-country naming creates challenges. A 'Polly' in one record may be 'Mary' in another. A 'Jim Davis' in the 1830 census may be 'James Davis' in a deed. The clustering of common surnames (Davis, Williams, Moore, Hall) in small communities means that naming patterns are essential for distinguishing individuals. The presence of identical names in multiple generations within the same community is the norm, not the exception.

naming_conventions.description: Welsh naming followed distinct patterns: patronymic origins (Jones = son of John, Williams = son of William, Davis = son of David, Evans = son of Evan) meant that surname clustering in the Welsh community was extreme — a township with fifty families might have fifteen named Jones and ten named Williams. Given names were often distinctly Welsh: Evan, Owen, Griffith, Cadwaladr (male); Gwenllian, Myfanwy, Elen (female). The Americanization of Welsh names happened gradually — a 'Gruffydd' became 'Griffith' became 'Griffin.' Welsh patronymic naming makes it essential to use given names, not surnames, as the primary identifier.
naming_conventions.genealogical_significance: The extreme surname clustering in Welsh communities is both a challenge and a tool. A Jones in Jackson County's Jackson Township is almost certainly Welsh. But distinguishing one Jones from another requires given names, birth dates, and church records. Welsh church records — which used given names as primary identifiers — are essential for this research. The Welsh tradition of naming children after grandparents was followed with precision.

foodways.food_tradition: Cornbread and its variants — the staple of hill-country life
foodways.context: Cornbread in southeastern Ohio was not a side dish — it was the foundation of the diet. Corn grew on the thin hillside soils where wheat could not. Cornbread, corn pone, corn mush (fried or boiled), hominy, and grits were daily foods for every meal. The corn was ground at the hollow's grist mill — the mill was as important as the church. White cornmeal (not yellow) was the standard in southeastern Ohio, a preference inherited from the Virginia-Kentucky migration. Cornbread was baked in a cast-iron skillet over an open fire or in a wood-fired oven.
foodways.class_signal: Cornbread was universal across the hill-country population. The type of corn preparation — cornbread for daily meals, fine wheat bread for Sunday or holidays — was a modest class signal. A family that ate wheat bread regularly was doing well; a family that ate cornbread exclusively was not.
foodways.narrative_hook: Cornbread, three meals a day — {ancestor_name} ate it because corn grew on the hillside where wheat would not, and the grist mill down the creek was the only industry the hollow had that never failed.

foodways.food_tradition: Ramps and wild greens — the hunger-gap food
foodways.context: Ramps (wild leeks), poke sallet, dandelion greens, and other wild plants gathered from the forest floor were critical food during the spring hunger gap — the weeks between the exhaustion of winter stores and the first garden harvest. Ramps were the first green thing available in spring, emerging in March-April in the rich forest soils of north-facing slopes. The smell of ramps cooking — pungent, garlicky, overwhelming — was the smell of April in the hollows. Poke sallet was boiled twice (to remove toxins) and fried with fatback. These were not nostalgic folk foods — they were survival foods that kept families alive when the cellar was empty.
foodways.class_signal: Eating ramps and wild greens was a poverty signal. Families with enough stored food and early garden production did not need to gather wild plants. Ramp gatherers were the families closest to the edge.
foodways.narrative_hook: The ramps came up in March, and {ancestor_name} gathered them because the cellar was empty, the garden was not ready, and the forest floor was the only grocery store that did not charge interest.

foodways.context: Pawpaw trees grew wild along creek bottoms throughout southeastern Ohio. The custard-like fruit ripened in September-October and was gathered, eaten fresh, or made into pudding and bread. Pawpaws could not be stored or shipped — they were intensely local food, eaten within days of picking. The pawpaw festival tradition (informal gatherings to eat the ripening fruit) was a southeastern Ohio cultural event. Athens County would later formalize this as the Ohio Pawpaw Festival.
foodways.class_signal: Pawpaw gathering was universal in the hill country — a free food that required only the labor of walking to the creek bottom. No class distinction.
foodways.narrative_hook: Pawpaw season was the one time the creek bottom fed the family for free — {ancestor_name} gathering the soft, custard-sweet fruit from the trees along the water, eating what the forest gave because the farm never gave enough.

foodways.food_tradition: Hog killing and the preserved-pork economy
foodways.context: Hogs fattened on forest mast (acorns and beechnuts) were the primary protein source in southeastern Ohio's hill country. Hog killing in late November-December was the central food event of the year — the entire animal processed: hams cured in salt and smoke, sausage ground, lard rendered, head cheese from the head, chitlins cleaned. The number of hogs a family could keep was a measure of food security. The hogs ran semi-wild in the forest until fall, when they were penned and fattened on corn for the killing.
foodways.class_signal: Hog ownership was the primary measure of food self-sufficiency in the hill country. A family with six hogs was well-fed; a family with two was on the edge; a family with none was dependent on the store.
foodways.narrative_hook: Hog killing day in {county} County was when the year's protein was decided — {ancestor_name} in the cold November air, the hog scalded and scraped, every piece saved because the smoke-house in January was the only thing between the family and cornbread alone.

dialect_markers.feature: Appalachian English north of the river
dialect_markers.description: The hill-country dialect of southeastern Ohio was Appalachian English — indistinguishable from the speech of eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia. 'Fire' was one syllable ('far'). 'Holler' for hollow. 'Poke' for paper bag. 'Might could' as a double modal. 'You'uns' or 'you all.' The 'a-prefix' on present participles: 'a-going,' 'a-coming.' This dialect was the speech of the Virginia-Kentucky migration stream and it persisted in southeastern Ohio because the isolation that created it in Appalachia was replicated on the Ohio side of the river. The Ohio River was not a dialect boundary — the same speech patterns existed on both banks.
dialect_markers.class_signal: Appalachian dialect was a powerful stigma marker in Ohio. When southeastern Ohio families migrated to Columbus, Dayton, or Cleveland, their speech immediately identified them as 'hillbillies' — a class marker that affected employment, housing, and social acceptance. The dialect was the single most visible cultural marker of Appalachian origin in the destination cities.
dialect_markers.narrative_hook: When {ancestor_name} spoke in Columbus, the accent said everything before the words did — the flat vowels, the double modals, the 'holler' instead of 'hollow' that told every listener exactly where {pronoun_subject} came from and exactly where {pronoun_subject} stood.

dialect_markers.feature: New England speech patterns in Marietta
dialect_markers.description: The Ohio Company settlers brought New England speech patterns to Marietta — the 'r-dropping' of eastern Massachusetts, the broad 'a' in 'bath' and 'dance.' These patterns persisted in Marietta's educated class for two to three generations before blending into the general Midwestern speech of the region. In the early settlement period, the contrast between Marietta's New England speech and the surrounding hill country's Virginia-Kentucky dialect was one of the sharpest dialect boundaries in the United States.
dialect_markers.class_signal: New England speech in Marietta signaled education, institutional authority, and the Ohio Company elite. The dialect boundary between Marietta and the surrounding hill country was also a class boundary.
dialect_markers.narrative_hook: The way {ancestor_name} spoke told Marietta everything — whether the vowels came from Massachusetts or Virginia, whether the family had come down the river with the Ohio Company or up the hollows from the south. The dialect was the first census anyone took.

dialect_markers.description: Welsh was a living language in Jackson County into the 1920s. Church services conducted in Welsh. Eisteddfod competitions held in Welsh. Gravestone inscriptions in Welsh. The Welsh-language newspaper Y Drych (published in Utica, NY) circulated among the Jackson County Welsh community. The transition from Welsh to English happened gradually — the children of immigrants were bilingual; the grandchildren spoke English with Welsh phrases retained in the family vocabulary. Welsh-language documents in Jackson County records (church minutes, correspondence) require Welsh-language literacy to use.
dialect_markers.class_signal: Welsh language use signaled the ethnic enclave — not a class marker per se, but a community boundary. Welsh-speaking families married within the community; the shift to English marked the beginning of assimilation into the broader population.
dialect_markers.narrative_hook: The hymns at Horeb Chapel in Jackson County were in Welsh until the grandchildren stopped understanding the words — {ancestor_name} could worship in the language of Cardiganshire, compete at the eisteddfod in the language of the bards, and read the gravestone of {pronoun_possessive} grandfather in the language it was carved.

dialect_markers.description: The coal camps in Athens, Meigs, and Vinton counties were multilingual environments. Welsh, Irish English, Hungarian, Polish, Italian, and Appalachian English coexisted in the same mine and the same camp. Underground, miners developed a working vocabulary that crossed language barriers — hand signals, mining terminology, and a pidgin English that served basic communication. Aboveground, ethnic groups maintained separate languages in churches, homes, and fraternal organizations. The company store conducted business in English, but storekeepers who spoke multiple languages were valued.
dialect_markers.class_signal: Language diversity in the coal camps was a class marker in the negative — it signaled the extractive-industry working class to outsiders. Within the camps, language maintained ethnic boundaries and social networks.
dialect_markers.narrative_hook: Underground, {ancestor_name} worked beside men who spoke Welsh, Hungarian, and a version of English that came from Kentucky — they communicated by lamplight and hand signal, and the coal did not care what language the man with the pick was thinking in.

social_hierarchies.description: Southeastern Ohio had a distinct social hierarchy that varied between sub-regions: (1) In Washington County: the Ohio Company elite — descendants of New England settlers, professionals, and merchants connected to Marietta College and the institutional establishment. (2) In the river towns: merchants, lawyers, ironmasters (antebellum), and industrialists who controlled commerce. (3) In the hill country: yeoman farmers who owned their land — a modest but respected class. (4) Landless tenants and wage laborers — the poorest white population, dependent on iron furnaces, coal mines, or day labor. (5) The small Black population — free Blacks in river towns, confined to the lowest-paying labor and socially marginalized. (6) In the coal camps: a further stratification by ethnicity — native-born white miners at the top, Welsh miners (respected for skill), Irish miners, and Southern/Eastern European immigrants at the bottom. The coal operator sat above all of them. The ironmasters of the antebellum period were the wealthiest individuals in the region — their mansions in Ironton reflected a class gap as extreme as any in the South.
social_hierarchies.narrative_hook: The ironmaster's mansion on the bluff above the river, the yeoman's cabin on the ridge, and the landless laborer's shack in the hollow — {ancestor_name} knew exactly where {pronoun_subject} stood in southeastern Ohio's class ladder, and the distance between the rungs was measured in acres, education, and the accident of which side of the Ohio River {pronoun_possessive} people had crossed from.

material_culture.item: The hill-country cabin (log and later frame)
material_culture.description: The standard hill-country dwelling was a single-pen or double-pen log cabin — one or two rooms, dirt or puncheon floor, stick-and-mud chimney, no glass windows (oiled paper or wooden shutters). By the mid-nineteenth century, frame additions were common — a lean-to kitchen, a loft for sleeping. The cabin sat on a small cleared patch in the hollow or on the ridge, surrounded by forest. The material quality of the cabin was a direct measure of the family's prosperity: a family that could afford sawn lumber, glass windows, and a stone chimney was doing well. A family still in a chinked-log cabin with a dirt floor in 1880 was not.
material_culture.class_signal: Log cabin construction persisted in southeastern Ohio's hill country decades after it had been replaced by frame construction elsewhere in Ohio. A log cabin in the 1870s or 1880s was a poverty marker — the family could not afford sawn lumber. Frame houses in the river towns and county seats signaled middle-class prosperity.
material_culture.narrative_hook: The cabin in the hollow was one room, a loft, and a stick chimney — {ancestor_name} built it from the trees that stood on the land because the land was the only building material the hollow provided for free.

material_culture.description: Coal camp housing in southeastern Ohio was similar to but generally smaller and less substantial than SC Piedmont mill village housing. A typical coal camp house was a three-to-four-room frame structure on posts (not piers), single-wall construction, coal-heated, company-owned. The houses were built quickly and maintained minimally. Some camps had better housing — the larger Hocking Valley operations provided more substantial homes — but the standard was spartan. The company owned the house and could evict at will.
material_culture.class_signal: Living in a coal camp house signaled the mining working class. The contrast with the operator's house (typically a substantial frame dwelling on higher ground) was as stark as the mill owner/mill worker divide in the SC Piedmont.
material_culture.narrative_hook: The company house at the mine was three rooms and belonged to the company — {ancestor_name} could live there as long as {pronoun_subject} worked the face, and the eviction notice was as close as the next strike or the next disagreement with the boss.

cultural_calendar.timing: Various — typically summer or early fall
cultural_calendar.description: The eisteddfod — a competitive festival of poetry, singing, and oratory conducted in Welsh — was the defining cultural event of the Jackson County Welsh community. Competitions in penillion singing (singing to harp accompaniment), choral music, poetry composition, and recitation maintained Welsh cultural identity across generations. The eisteddfod tradition was transplanted directly from Wales and continued in Jackson County into the early twentieth century, though the gradual shift from Welsh to English eventually transformed it from a Welsh-language event into an English-language cultural celebration.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: The eisteddfod was the primary mechanism for maintaining Welsh identity in Ohio. It transmitted the Welsh language, musical tradition, and literary culture to new generations. Its decline in the early twentieth century marks the assimilation of the Welsh community into the broader American population.

cultural_calendar.description: The Baptist and Methodist camp meeting/revival tradition was the primary communal religious event in the hill country and later in the coal camps. Week-long evening services with a visiting evangelist drew families from across the hollow for worship, socializing, and courtship. The camp meeting tradition in southeastern Ohio was identical to the tradition in eastern Kentucky and southern West Virginia — brought by the same Virginia-Kentucky migration stream that settled the region.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: Revival was the one event that gathered the dispersed hill-country population into a single place. It served social functions — courtship, news exchange, community renewal — as much as religious ones. In the coal camps, revival provided emotional release from the mine's relentless demands.

cultural_calendar.description: Decoration Day (later Memorial Day) held particular significance in southeastern Ohio because of the region's Civil War service and its mining disasters. Cemetery decorations honored both war dead and mine disaster victims. In small communities, Decoration Day was the annual gathering at the family cemetery — the one day when families who had scattered returned to the home place to clean graves, place flowers, and reconnect. This tradition persisted in southeastern Ohio well past its decline in urban areas.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: Decoration Day was the annual homecoming — the day the out-migration reversed briefly as families returned to the hollows. For families who had moved to Columbus or Dayton, the drive back for Decoration Day was the strongest remaining connection to the home place.

cultural_calendar.description: The communal apple butter boiling was a fall social event that combined food preservation with community gathering. Families brought apples to a central location (often a farmstead with a large copper kettle) and took turns stirring the thick, cinnamon-scented butter over an open fire for hours. The stirring required continuous attention — the paddle was passed from person to person through the day. Apple butter was preserved in crocks and jars and lasted through the winter. The smell of apple butter boiling — sweet, spiced, and smoky — was the quintessential fall scent of southeastern Ohio.
cultural_calendar.social_significance: Apple butter boilings were work events that functioned as social gatherings — similar to corn shuckings and barn raisings. They required communal labor and rewarded it with communal celebration. The tradition survived in southeastern Ohio longer than in most of the eastern United States.

narrative_hooks: Southeastern Ohio was two cultures sharing one landscape — the Ohio Company settlers at Marietta with their college and their courthouse and their Congregational church, and the Virginia hill farmers in the hollows with their corn patches and their Baptist revivals. {ancestor_name} belonged to one stream or the other, and the difference was audible in the first sentence {pronoun_subject} spoke.

The Welsh chapel in Jackson County was a piece of Cardiganshire transplanted to Ohio — {ancestor_name} could worship in Welsh, compete at the eisteddfod, and be buried under a Welsh inscription, three thousand miles from the country that shaped the language.
The hill-country cabin was one room, a loft, and a chimney — {ancestor_name} built it from what the hollow provided and lived in it because the hollow provided nothing else.

When {ancestor_name} spoke in Columbus, the Appalachian accent said 'hillbilly' before the words said anything else — the same speech that was normal in the hollow was stigma in the city, and the price of leaving was learning to hide the voice that raised you.

Ramps in March, pawpaws in September, and cornbread year-round — {ancestor_name}'s diet was the hollow's diet, shaped by what the thin hillside soil would grow and what the forest floor would give away for free.

The eisteddfod in Jackson County was poetry and song in Welsh — a tradition older than Ohio, older than America, carried across the ocean in the same trunk that carried the family Bible and the address of the cousin who had gone before.
source_references: Hildreth, Samuel P. Pioneer History: Being an Account of the First Examinations of the Ohio Valley. Cincinnati: H. W. Derby, 1848.
Cayton, Andrew R. L. The Frontier Republic: Ideology and Politics in the Ohio Country, 1780-1825. Kent, OH: Kent State University Press, 1986.
Knowles, Anne Kelly. Calvinists Incorporated: Welsh Immigrants on Ohio's Industrial Frontier. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1997.
Fischer, David Hackett. Albion's Seed: Four British Folkways in America. New York: Oxford University Press, 1989.
Eller, Ronald D. Miners, Millhands, and Mountaineers: Industrialization of the Appalachian South, 1880-1930. Knoxville: University of Tennessee Press, 1982.
Federal Writers' Project. The Ohio Guide. New York: Oxford University Press, 1940.
Thomas, R. D. Hanes Cymry America (History of the Welsh in America). Utica, NY: T. J. Griffiths, 1872.
